speaker: interviewer
text: This is slate number 3 of the interview with Joe Dispeker. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Could you tell me a little bit about how you got your family to the United States in 1938?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was-- took a long time. It wasn't a normal procedure. I had to send my parents an affidavit as a son. It was accepted. Also, there was very little money there, that a son would take care of his parents. And I was already-- had already applied for American citizenship. So my affidavit would have been good enough for my parents. And they did get eventually the visa and waited for-- for years, from 1938-- from-- for-- actually, they had applied for it earlier. To get the visa, I don't know exactly now when it was, but it took a long time. But they had the visa. In fact, they were-- they were scheduled to sail in December 1938 on the to America when my father was arrested. And that, of course, ended his chance of-- to-- to live and to come to America. But my mother took that boat. She came. And my uncle, who had survived, took charge of the things that my mother could not finish as far as the financial arrangements and moving arrangements and these things were concerned. And she came by herself. I could pick her up on the-- at the pier in New York on the-- December 23, 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she allowed to bring any-- anything with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My-- my parents were allowed to bring-- that was-- because it was already arranged before-- before November 9. See, you that-- that was the-- the dividing line. After November 9, 1938, they were not allowed to bring anything but maybe a-- a little handbag and $10. But my mother was still allowed to bring-- to bring my parent's furniture and possessions, so practical possessions. They were totally stripped of their money because, first of all, they had to pay for everything that they-- that they took. They had to pay it over again, which I believe I have already mentioned. And then another thing. It was after 1938, after November 1938, there was a-- a new tax called Juden [INAUDIBLE], the Jewish tax, which was supposed to be a penalty for the murder of-- of the Jewish-- of-- of the German ambassador or attache ambassador in Paris, that took whatever they had left away from them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there was a very, very small amount of money left on a so-called closed account that had to be left in Germany. It could not be taken out, and she came without any money. And-- but at least she was there, and that was all that counted-- that was counted at the moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was a broken woman. She had just lost her husband in such a horrible way. And not only her husband, but everybody was in jeopardy, all her-- her brothers and sisters, the-- the whole family. All the news that she brought was absolutely dismal about the people that were murdered on Kristallnacht or beaten so badly that they died, the ones who committed suicide. It was a story of one tragedy after the other. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your life after your mother arrived in the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after my mother arrived in the United States, we lived together. I had just gotten married. And the three of us lived together in an apartment in Upper Manhattan, which was called the Third Reich because there were most of the Jews had lived there-- actually, the Fourth Reich. The Third Reich was the Nazi Reich. And they called it the Fourth Reich because all the immigrant Jews lived in Washington Heights, which was, at that time, probably a cheap area of New York. And also old houses where they could put-- where they had this big German furniture. They could continue their German life. And in a way, you could go to the grocery store and speak German, because the-- the-- the old grandmothers and grandfathers that came along didn't speak English. So you could go and buy groceries and not-- not be-- not necessary to speak English. So that-- that's-- that's where we lived. And we had jobs, my wife and I, and I didn't make any headway. And then when I finally got an opportunity to go to California-- we were always advised, leave New York. New York is-- you can't make any headway in New York. America is a big country. Get out and go somewhere else. There are much better opportunities. And I had a chance to go to California through a business associate of my father, a friend whom-- with whom he had worked for many years and who wanted a younger man to help him in his newly-established business in Los Angeles. And he asked me to come and join him. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this before you were in the Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So I-- I bought an old, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] used Chevrolet and drove to California, and I saw the majesty of America, the big country. It was a real experience to drive from New York all the way to California in those days.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm?

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1940, 1940. I listened mostly on the radio, and I heard about the Blitzkrieg, how-- how-- about the horror of the bombings of London that that was. I remember that came into the-- my-- one part of my experience of traveling to America-- through America. But it was a great experience to go through Nebraska and Iowa and to see these endless, endless roads. And it gave me the feeling that no matter what Hitler does, he's not going to conquer the world. And in America, then I came to Los Angeles and started working. This friend of my father's was representing some Eastern textile mills, selling to the garment industry in Los Angeles. And I joined him, and I started going around and selling fabrics to the garment manufacturers in Los Angeles. I did this until I retired.

speaker: interviewer
text: Weren't you in the Army at one point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That was-- that was interruption of my-- but yeah, in 1944, I went into the Army. And thanks to my previous education and my German language, I got certain assignments that were, I hope, useful to the United States Army. I-- I-- I went into the Air Force. And then when I got into the Air Force, I was assigned to their intelligence department. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we were in a-- in a-- in Florida at MacDill Field, and I was trained for [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] radio intelligence, which meant we were supposed to broadcast propaganda to the German troops in German. Like saying, you better get-- go-- give up. Go home to mother. The war is over, you know, and this sort of thing. And we had very expensive equipment, actually. We told-- they were told-- we were told that each truck-- of our broadcasting truck was  worth $25,000, which was, in those days, money. And we were all ready to go to Europe when my truck was colliding with a-- a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a big truck, a big truck on the main street of the MacDill Field camp, and we were thrown off. I was thrown off. The lieutenant was-- was-- was-- I was a-- a sergeant at that time-- and the lieutenant was thrown off. And I was taken to a hospital, and my-- my unit left without me. And after I-- they found out that I had a broken disk in my back and that they-- they would not be able to send me to Europe. And then shortly after that, I was dismissed from the Army. So I missed the chance to be part of the ending of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened in your life after the end of the war, in terms of looking for-- I'm sure you tried to find members of your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, naturally.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: As soon as the war was over, while we had [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] rather [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] dark feelings about it, we knew that there was something terrible had happened, but it was much worse than we ever thought. We found out what happened to the various members. My mother's oldest sister had died in Theresienstadt. We got a letter from-- from another person that witnessed it through the Red Cross that she had died. And shortly after that, her son, who had already come to America, was so upset and so-- about his mother's fate and about that-- the whole situation, that even so he was in America, he could still not get used to the freedom of life here. He was always looking over his shoulder, feeling that-- he had a regular persecution complex, and finally, he poisoned himself. And my mother's younger sister, who had lived in her father's house, she was still there together with the older one. She was so-- in such fear of the deportation that in her desperation she poured gasoline over her bed and burned herself to death. And my mother's younger brother, who lived in Hamburg and who was married to a German woman, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] also before he was arrested and to be taken, shipped to the East, shot himself. So there was nobody left in the family. And in the wider family, I could tell endless stories of the people. My father's sister, was-- my father's only sister, was arrested in Munich, taken in a-- in a carload to the East. And what we heard is that that carload never arrived in-- in Auschwitz, or wherever the Polish camp was that she was supposed to be taken to, that the  people were just left there to-- to-- to die in the car, to starve to death or freeze to death.

speaker: interviewee
text: So [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] this is an endless story, that I could go on and on if I would really want to remember it all, that the family was totally wiped out. Anybody who was-- had not gotten out was finished. How did this affect you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we learned to cope with the-- with things. In the beginning, as I said, even-- even in America, we were still shy and fearful and-- and-- and distrusting. Eventually, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we try to live with  it. We have nightmares. In our dreams, it comes back to us. And there is always one feeling-- a feeling of guilt that we didn't do enough to get people out. The feeling that we were selected to lead a full life while all those other people who were every bit as deserving as we, or as undeserving as we are, could not even begin to lead a life. It was not only the old ones. There were so many young people that never had a chance. And that guilt feeling, it may be unjustified because-- well, we did what we could to help, but we did not do enough. We were also occupied in America with making a living for ourselves. And we could not devote 100% of our time to get other people out. But at least I must say that I got my parents out, at least my mother. And I helped quite a few people, other people, to get out, friends. And I gave affidavits myself, and I-- I-- I went around to other people to get them for them. But it's-- it's-- it's still not enough. And I can only imagine how the people must feel who went out to America and left their parents behind. I-- I can just sympathize with them. It's something that is just too-- too terrible to think about. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You have a daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we have a daughter. Her name is Louise, and she was born in Hollywood. And we never really discussed the things that had happened with her. We-- we tried somehow to spare her, or we didn't have-- we didn't feel like talking about it. She grew up as a real American child, and made her career as a teacher. And she lives in Canyon Country and has two sons. And they're working out all right. They are normal kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have some pictures you'd like to show us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I do.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Here we go. We're on.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife, Irma.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long have you been married?

speaker: interviewee
text: 40 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Irma, how many children do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have one daughter, Louise Goudchaux.

speaker: interviewer
text: And any grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two grandsons, one 20 years and one 22 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Joe, if you had a message to leave with those grandkids, what would it be? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my message would really be enjoy your free life in America, but remember that those terrible things can happen anywhere. And never forget your roots. Never forget that you are Jews and that you have to keep up the traditions for which your family has suffered so much.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother and her-- her sister and a younger brother. And the sister and the younger brother, I told you how they-- how terribly they ended. But this is my mother as a young girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name-- it was Elsbeth Friedmann and Martha Friedmann and Hugo Friedmann.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, starting on the left, who's-- who's the--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Hugo Friedman.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And?

speaker: interviewee
text: And Martha Friedman and Elsbeth Friedman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture must have been taken-- well, I guess my mother would probably be, I would say 1885.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in Kurten [GERMAN] in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother and father with me, probably taken in 1908.

speaker: interviewer
text: The names?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name is-- my mother Elsbeth and Sigmond Dispeker, and I was, at that time, Joachim Dispeker.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, great.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the year?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Furth in Bavaria.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me, Joe Dispeker about 1918-- 1916. My father had that picture with him in the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Furth in 1916, and I was eight years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our daughter Louise, Louise Jeanette Dispeker Goudchaux. And this is-- was taken about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] four or five years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what year, do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: Will be 19-- what is it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: They have the wrong shape, huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, that's OK. And so what is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the grave of my grandparents in Furth, Joseph and Laura Dispeker. And it shows-- that they-- how the metal plates that you see on this picture, were taken off and were taken away during the war when the country needed metal badly. And when we found the grave years later, it was barren, without-- and stripped of any name or any-- any way of recognizing who is buried there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year do you think this picture was taken in?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was probably taken in-- in the 1920s, but when-- when-- when-- when-- when-- when everything was still in good shape.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the same grave the way it is now after we had the names put on again by a-- by a-- what do you call them?

speaker: interviewer
text: Engraver?

speaker: interviewee
text: By engraver, yes, into the stone directly. But at least it looks civilized now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe-- maybe can-- wouldn't you  like it better a little larger and the less to-- less of the body?

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. These are our grandsons Michael and Adam Goudchaux. Michael is now 22 years old. Adam is 20. And they-- Michael lives in Waterloo, Iowa, where he is a fireman. And Adam goes to college in the-- Los Angeles to Junior Valley College.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken two years ago in 1994.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my great-grandfather, Isaac Simon Dispeker, who was born in 1809 in Baiersdorf and died in Furth in 1886. He was the founder of our family business, IS Dispeker a company in which four generations of Dispekers were employed. He had seven children. And these paintings were made in various forms and sizes for all seven children. Only, I think, three sets of these pictures are still in existence.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and who is this?

speaker: interviewer
text: Here. Wait, don't, don't, don't--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my great-grandmother, Jeanette Dispeker. She was born on the 18th of October, 1818, which was the day of the battle of-- the famous Battle of Leipzig in which Napoleon was defeated. So you have an idea how-- about the time frame. I don't know exactly when she died. She died in the 1870s, quite a while-- about 10 years before her husband. And she was the mother of seven kids. And as you see, she was an Orthodox Jewish woman who is wearing a sheitel.
